key_insight: HPG-Diff combines physics-guided diffusion with a differentiable connectivity constraint to generate topology designs that are both load-path aware and less prone to floating material.
engineering_relevance: The method aims to improve topology optimization generalization across unseen boundary conditions while producing more physically usable structural layouts.
method: Hierarchical physics guidance aligns precomputed physics features with the denoising steps to steer material toward optimal load paths.
A floating material suppression loss uses a virtual heat-propagation analogy to penalize disconnected material during training.
Lightweight LoRA fine-tuning is used to adapt the model to non-square rectangular domains with limited data.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports average compliance errors of 0.87% in-distribution and 5.29% out-of-distribution, with floating material ratios reduced to 2.90% and 2.44%, respectively.
watch_item: The connectivity constraint and domain adaptation results are promising, but performance may still depend on how well the physics features, load conditions, and fine-tuning setup transfer to new geometries.
paper_title: HPG-Diff: Hierarchical physics-guided diffusion with differentiable connectivity constraints for topology optimization
